title: Bond Price Fragility and the Structure of the Mutual Fund Industry
abstract: We conjecture that mutual funds with large shares of outstanding bond issues are more inclined to internalize the negative price spillovers of fire sales and thus sell their holdings in those issues, to a lower extent, when they experience redemptions. We provide evidence consistent with this conjecture and further show that ownership concentration limits bonds’ exposures to flow-induced fire sales. We exploit variation in negative spillovers arising from the Fed’s SMCCF to confirm the economic mechanism and explore our findings’ implications for fund performance and fire-sale spillovers to other funds.

author.Mariassunta Giannetti*: Stockholm School of Economics, Sweden, CEPR, and ECGI

article.1 Conceptual Framework and Testable Impl:
Issue Section: Article Editor: Itay Goldstein Itay Goldstein Editor Search for other works by this author on: Oxford Academic Google Scholar

Mutual funds’ increased role within the corporate bond market has raised concerns about financial stability.1Bond mutual funds invest in illiquid assets but issue liquid liabilities, which their investors can redeem on demand. When large redemptions occur, these funds may be forced to sell their holdings at fire-sale prices. Fire sales in turn can amplify the initial shock, leading to more redemptions and sales because fire-sale prices negatively affect the performance of other funds holding the same securities (Falato et al. 2021). Vicious cycles, in which redemptions lead to fire sales and fire sales lead to more redemptions, are particularly worrisome in the case of bond mutual funds, because low fund returns have been shown to generate outflows at an increasing rate (Goldstein, Jiang, and Ng 2017) and could, through overlapping portfolios, engender widespread contagion (Ibragimov, Jaffee, and Walden 2011;Wagner 2011).

The extent of contagion, however, depends on how bond mutual funds liquidate their portfolios when redemptions occur. Negative spillovers are believed to be strongest if funds reduce their positions pro rata and are mitigated if funds hold cash and liquidate their liquid holdings first (Morris, Shim, and Shin 2017). It has been largely neglected that funds may also internalize the spillovers of their sales. Specifically, intermediaries that own a large proportion of a bond issue may internalize that their sales will drive down the price of that issue and hence the value of the unsold part of their position. As a result, when facing negative shocks, funds may have an incentive to limit the sales of bond issues in which they own large shares in order to attempt to stabilize the bond’s price. We not only provide evidence supporting this hypothesis but also explore the consequences of bond mutual funds’ strategic behavior on the cross-section of bond returns and the second-round spillovers of fire sales.

Consistent with our hypothesis, we show that when faced with large redemptions at the fund or industry levels, bond mutual funds decrease their positions in bond issues of which they hold a large share to a lower extent. The effects are stronger among high-yield bonds and more illiquid bonds, for which any sales can be expected to have larger negative spillovers on prices. We show that these trading patterns are more pronounced among funds that hold less cash and are forced to choose which corporate bonds to liquidate to meet redemptions. In addition, we consider funds that belong to families with a large stake in the same bond through their other funds. We show that these funds with particularly strong incentives to internalize the price effects on the unsold part of the position sell the bond to a lower extent. Not only do these mechanism tests support our hypotheses but also our estimates are robust when we control nonparametrically for bond-specific or fund-specific time-varying characteristics. Our results are equally invariant when we rely on exogenous changes in funds’ ownership shares due to fund mergers.

The effects of bond mutual funds’ stabilizing behavior are apparent in the cross-section of bond returns. We show for the first time that bond issues with more concentrated mutual fund ownership, as measured by the ownership share of the largest mutual fund owner, experience less pronounced price declines in periods of fire sales and are less exposed to outflows from the bond mutual fund industry.2The results are again more pronounced among high-yield bonds and are robust if we exploit exogenous changes in ownership concentration due to fund mergers or within-issuer differences in ownership concentration, indicating that differences in fundamentals do not drive our findings.

The cross-sectional tests on bond returns indicate that ownership concentration affects the distribution of fire sales. But does bond funds’ strategic behavior improve overall financial stability? We show that funds’ stabilizing trading behavior is associated with smaller negative spillovers. First, funds whose portfolios comprise a larger fraction of positions representing large shares of outstanding bond issues (henceforth, HIGHLARGEFRAC funds) perform better, especially in periods of turmoil. Second, arguably thanks to the fact that investors rationally expect better performance, HIGHLARGEFRAC funds’ outflows exhibit lower correlation with negative performance. These two channels enhance the asset stability of HIGHLARGEFRAC funds and limit second-round redemptions, thus mitigating the effects of the initial redemption shock for all funds with overlapping portfolios.

Moreover, as we show with the help of a simple framework, HIGHLARGEFRAC funds do not need to destabilize the prices of the bonds they sell to meet redemptions because they sell the securities they hold in small amounts. Consistent with this conjecture, we find that even when we consider common holdings of securities in which HIGHLARGEFRAC funds have smaller positions (ie, the securities they sell to a larger extent), the negative spillovers of redemptions for peer funds are not larger than those from other funds that do not engage in the stabilizing behavior we highlight. Thus, the attempts of HIGHLARGEFRAC funds to reduce the negative spillovers of their trades not only benefit their own performance and affect the distribution of fire sales but also improve the stability of the whole mutual fund industry by reducing second-round price impacts and the vicious cycle of fire sales.

We further evaluate the relevance of our hypothesis, exploiting additional empirical implications created by the Fed’s intervention during the COVID-19 turmoil in the corporate bond market. During March 2020, bond mutual funds experienced unprecedented redemptions (Falato, Goldstein, and Hortaçsu 2021). On March 23, 2020, the Fed pledged to purchase investment-grade corporate bonds with less than 5-year maturity through the Secondary Market Corporate Credit Facility (SMCCF).3With its announcement, the Fed provided a price backstop and made the demand for eligible securities far more elastic (see, e.g.,Haddad, Moreira, and Muir 2021;Kargar et al. 2021;O’Hara and Zhou 2021). This allows us to test whether the desire to limit the negative price spillovers indeed drives the stabilizing trading behavior of bond funds and its consequent effects on bond returns.

We explore how funds’ stabilizing trading behavior and the effect of ownership concentration on bond returns vary, in response to the Fed’s intervention, distinguishing between SMCCF-eligible investment-grade bonds, ineligible investment-grade bonds, and ineligible high-yield bonds. We conjecture that the stabilizing trading behavior had little reason to persist for eligible bonds after the Fed’s intervention had made their demand more elastic.4We find that after the Fed’s announcement, the stabilizing trading behavior was indeed concentrated in bonds not eligible for the Fed’s purchases. These findings support our hypothesis that the internalization of negative price spillovers drives funds’ behavior.

As in our earlier tests, mutual funds’ trading behavior is reflected in bond returns. After the Fed’s announcement, consistent with the shift in the funds’ stabilizing trading behavior to ineligible securities, ownership concentration is positively related only to the returns of ineligible investment-grade and high-yield bonds that suffered flow-induced fire sales. All these patterns are robust if we limit unobserved heterogeneity by concentrating on investment-grade bonds with maturities in a close interval around the 5-year eligibility cutoff. This helps to rule out a subtler alternative that due to various market developments and policy interventions, funds traded short- and long-maturity bonds differently.

Taken together, these results support our interpretation that funds with a large share of an outstanding bond issue internalize the negative spillovers of their trades and that their behavior shields the returns of bonds with concentrated ownership from negative liquidity shocks. In this respect, our findings imply that not only can institutions with stable, long-term liabilities ride out transitory dislocations in market prices (Chodorow-Reich, Ghent, and Haddad 2021) but also open-end funds trading strategically can insulate part of their portfolios from negative liquidity shocks. Our mechanism may also explain whyKoijen and Yogo (2019)find that higher asset price volatility is associated with ownership by small, not large, institutions. Since large market players internalize the impact of their trades on security prices, particularly for illiquid securities, such as corporate bonds, they may not be destabilizing asGabaix et al. (2006)andBen-David et al. (2021)argue in the context of equity markets.

This paper is related to several strands of the literature. First, we contribute to the literature documenting the extent to which mutual funds introduce fragility in the corporate bond market. While funds’ ability to sell liquid assets, including Treasuries and high-quality corporate bonds, limits fire sales (Jiang, Li, and Wang 2021;Ma, Xiao, and Zeng 2022), when aggregate uncertainty rises, bond funds appear to stop selling their liquid asset first and scale down their portfolio pro rata leading to outflow-induced fire sales (Jiang, Li, and Wang 2021) and further sales from institutional investors (Cai et al. 2019). We explore whether funds are strategic in choosing which illiquid assets to sell during turbulent times.

While most of the literature documents significant price distortions associated with redemptions,Choi et al. (2020)find little evidence that corporate bond mutual funds generate fire sales after controlling for time-varying issuer-level information, possibly because bond mutual funds hold large amounts of cash (Chernenko and Sunderam 2020). By considering ownership concentration, our paper can help to explain why evidence of fire sales following negative shocks to bond mutual funds is mixed.

We also contribute to a strand of the literature exploring how the ownership structure of an asset affects its price volatility.Greenwood and Thesmar (2011)show that ownership dispersion decreases volatility if the owners face uncorrelated liquidity shocks, which implies that ownership concentration generally increases volatility. We condition on periods in which many owners of a security experience liquidity needs and test whether the trades of concentrated owners (HIGHLARGEFRAC funds) are strategic. In this context, we show that ownership concentration decreases an asset’s exposure to negative liquidity shocks, controlling for the size of the shock affecting a fund. This is the case even if unconditionally ownership concentration increases volatility, implying that the effects we uncover are limited to periods with large redemptions.

Others have shown that institutions that are not exposed to redemptions, such as insurance companies, guarantee stable ownership and limit the exposure of equities (Cella, Ellul, and Giannetti 2013) and bonds (Barbosa and Ozdagli 2020;Coppola 2022) to market shocks. We show that concentrated ownership by open-end mutual funds can be stabilizing because fund managers trade strategically. The mechanism that we propose and test is particularly relevant for the corporate bond market because corporate bonds are illiquid and the negative externalities of distressed sales are potentially larger than in equity markets (Ellul, Jotikasthira, and Lundblad 2011). In addition, firms offer a myriad of small bond issues, and each bond issue has a capitalization that is much smaller than the equity market capitalization of a typical company. It is thus particularly relevant to explore whether fund managers internalize the negative effects of their sales on the price of the issues in which they own large stakes.

Finally, our paper is related to work showing that banks’ strategic behavior limits the negative spillovers of fire sales. Specifically, large-market-share banks have been shown to be less inclined to foreclose defaulting mortgages (Favara and Giannetti 2017) and more likely to extend loans to borrowers in industries in distress (Giannetti and Saidi 2019) in order to avoid the negative externalities associated with fire sales. The mechanism we highlight is also related, but distinct from portfolio pumping, which refers to asset managers’ purchases of securities they already hold to inflate the prices and short-term performance at the expense of long-term performance (Bhattacharyya and Nanda 2013;Wang 2021). In our case, fund managers’ choices of securities to sell also aim to affect the value of the positions they hold, but their behavior limits negative spillovers and undervaluation and, as we show, improves their own fund’s performance during periods of turmoil.
We present a stylized model to illustrate the effect of an asset’s ownership concentration on the extent of price dislocations when the owners of the asset experience redemptions and flow-driven fire sales occur. Our objective is to show that for a given amount of redemptions experienced by its owners, securities with concentrated ownership experience smaller price dislocations. The model builds onFavara and Giannetti (2017), who aim to illustrate that atomistic banks and banks that have retained a large share of the outstanding mortgages in a region have different propensities to foreclose, but it is adapted to a context in which intermediaries experience redemptions and optimally choose which securities to liquidate.

We assume that there is a mass N of identical securities, each outstanding in mass 1. Securities are held by atomistic investors. Thus, each security is held by a mass 1 of different investors. Each investor holds one security. 5 At t = 0 ⁠ , the price of all securities is equal to one, which we assume to be their fundamental value. At t = 1 ⁠ , a fire sale occurs if a fraction ρ ρ of investors holding the security experiences redemptions and is forced to sell it. In this case, the fire-sale price is below the fundamental value and reflects the aggregate sales, ρ ρ ⁠ : ρ P = 1 − h ρ .

The parameterh>0captures the price impact due to the sales. Put differently, the demand for each security is downward sloping to reflect that capital is slow to move and that the price needs to drop to attract other investors (Shleifer and Vishny 1992).

Now consider an additional security, which we label with an asterisk. This security is also outstanding in mass 1 and is identical in every respect to those described above. In particular, its owners also experience redemptions. This additional security however has concentrated ownership structure. Specifically, one fund holds a fractionξξof it, with the remaining part held by atomistic investors.

The larger owner with a shareξξof the security also owns a subset of the other securities, in which it is an atomistic investor. Its initial wealth isξξ+∫NI(n)dn⁠, whereI(n)is an indicator function that takes value equal to one if the fund holds securityn(in infinitesimal amount), and zero otherwise. Without loss of generality, we assume that the fund holds a subsetX⊂Nof the securities. Thus, its wealth isξξ+X⁠. We label this fund HIGHLARGEFRAC, because a portion of its wealth consists of a large share of an outstanding security issue.

A fire sale of the security with concentrated ownership occurs if a fractionρρof the atomistic investors experience redemptions and are forced to sell it and the large investor also experiences redemptions of amountR. The shock experienced by the large investor is of the same magnitude as the one experienced by the atomistic investors on average if (⁠ξρξ+X)ρ≡R.

The main feature of the HIGHLARGEFRAC fund is that holding more than an infinitesimal amount, it internalizes that the size of its sales, δ δ 1 ⁠ , will affect the security’s price at t = 1 ⁠ , which can be written as ξ ρ δ P * = 1 − h [ ( 1 − ξ ) ρ + δ 1 ] , where ξ ρ ( 1 − ξ ) ρ are the aggregate sales by atomistic investors.

Our objective is to explore how the HIGHLARGEFRAC fund liquidates its portfolio when it experiences redemptions. We assume that the fund aims to maximize its net wealth after meeting the redemptions, and thus solves the following problem: δ δ ξ ρ δ ξ δ ρ δ m a x δ 1 , δ 2 ≤ X [ 1 − h [ ( 1 − ξ ) ρ + δ 1 ] ] ( ξ − δ 1 ) + ( 1 − h ρ ) ( X − δ 2 ) , subject to the constraint that the value of the sales must be equal to the redemptions: ξ ρ δ δ ρ δ [ 1 − h [ ( 1 − ξ ) ρ + δ 1 ] ] δ 1 + ( 1 − h ρ ) δ 2 = R .

That is, the fund decides the amount of the securities in which it is atomistic to liquidate,δδ2,and the proportion of the security in which it can affect prices,δδ1,subject to the constraint that it needs to meet the redemptions. The HIGHLARGEFRAC fund internalizes the effect of its sales on the price of security *, while being atomistic it takes the prices of all the other securities as determined by the aggregate sales. Proposition 1 summarizes the solution of the above problem. SeeAppendix Afor the proof.

Proposition 1. During fire sales, holding constant the redemptions experienced by the owners of different securities, the HIGHLARGEFRAC fund sells a lower proportion of the security in which it holds a large share of the outstanding issue in comparison to other securities that it holds in small amounts.

Proposition 1 derives from the fact that there is a fundamental difference between atomistic investors and the HIGHLARGEFRAC fund when redemptions occur. Atomistic investors in an asset rationally consider the extent of the price dislocation beyond their control, because the aggregate sales of other market participants determine the fire-sale price. The small (infinitesimal) amount they sell is irrelevant. The HIGHLARGEFRAC fund behaves like any other atomistic investor when liquidating the securities in which it is one of the many dispersed owners but becomes strategic and considers the effect of its sale size on the price dislocation and the negative feedback effect on the sale proceeds and the unsold part of the position, when it owns a positive mass of the issue and is therefore a concentrated owner. This leads the HIGHLARGEFRAC fund to behave strategically and not to liquidate its portfolio pro rata.

Note that the dollar value of the sales of the large position may be larger than for the sales of small positions when a fund has exhausted the small positions to sell, simply because the small positions sales are constrained by their initial size. Thus, the implications of the model are for proportional sales, not for absolute amounts, also considering that funds may not sell all their small positions for reasons that are outside our simple framework, such as the desire to maintain portfolio diversification or expectations on the future performance of the security.

Proposition 2 lists the additional empirical implications that follow readily from Proposition 1 and the model’s assumptions.

Proposition 2. The behavior of HIGHLARGEFRAC funds has the following implications: First, securities with concentrated ownership tend to perform better in periods of fire sales because funds with large proportional ownership sell to a lower extent than atomistic investors. Second, the drop in the net asset value of a HIGHLARGEFRAC fund following forced asset sales is lower for given intensity of the redemption shock because the fund can shield part of its portfolio from the fire sales, thanks to its strategic behavior. The HIGHLARGEFRAC fund consequently experiences better performance than atomistic funds. Third, the atomistic funds that hold the security with concentrated ownership also experience better performance.

Finally, the model can be easily extended to consider funds that hold several large positions in outstanding bond issues and internalize their price impact in more than one security. Besides choosing how much of the positions in which they are atomistic to liquidate, once the small positions are exhausted, these funds have the option to partially liquidate their large positions. As proved in Proposition 1, the marginal effect of the sale of the large positionion the fund’s net wealth is:ξ−hiξi.For a givenhi,to limit the negative effect on its net wealth, the fund finds it optimal to liquidate positions in which it owns a relatively smaller shareξξi⁠, implying that Proposition 1 can be easily extended to noninfinitesimal positions.

For a givenξξi,the negative effect of the sales on the fund wealth att+1is thus positively related to the illiquidity of a security, as captured by the parameterhi⁠. This impliesCorollary 1.

Corollary 1. A fund that has to sell some of its large positions will sell to a larger extent the relatively liquid ones.

In what follows, we test whether funds that hold large positions in outstanding bond issues indeed trade differently from other funds during periods of generalized fire sales. We also test the implications of our hypothesis for bond returns and for the performance of HIGHLARGEFRAC funds and peer funds.
article.2 Data Sources and Descriptive Statistic:
2.1 Sample funds

We obtain data on bond mutual fund holdings from Morningstar, data on fund characteristics from Morningstar Direct and the CRSP Mutual Funds database, data on corporate bond characteristics from Mergent’s Fixed Income Securities Database (FISD), and data on corporate bond transactions from FINRA’s enhanced TRACE database. Our main sample covers the period from January 2003 to December 2019. When we consider the consequences of the Fed’s intervention following the COVID-19 shock, the sample period is April 2020 to September 2020. SeeAppendix Bfor detailed variable definitions.

Given our focus on the corporate bond market, our main sample includes 1,016 open-end mutual funds classified by Morningstar as taxable bond funds that have at least 50% of their portfolio in corporate bonds (of these, 484 invest mostly in investment-grade bonds, while 532 invest mostly in high-yield bonds; only seven are index funds). These funds have less liquid portfolios and are more likely to create concerns for financial stability than the average fixed income fund.

We construct a survivorship bias-free data set that includes information on a variety of fund characteristics, including total net assets under management (TNA), returns, flows, fees, and fund-level bond holdings. While the SEC requires mutual funds to report holdings on a quarterly basis, most funds voluntarily report their holdings more frequently. Approximately 74% of the fund-reporting period observations in our sample are monthly, while the remaining are quarterly. Our changes in position variables thus vary between funds based on reporting frequency. Omitting observations for funds that do not report monthly holdings leaves our results unaffected.

Table 1, panel A, reports descriptive statistics for various fund attributes, with the first five columns highlighting the main sample (42,251 fund-reporting period observations) and the last dedicated to the COVID-19 sample. Consistent with the growth in bond mutual funds documented byGoldstein, Jiang, and Ng (2017), our sample funds experience significant inflows. The average monthly fund flow is 0.6% of TNA, with the 10th and 90th percentiles at –3.0% and 5.2%, respectively, indicating significant variation across funds and over time.

The average fund holds 12.2% of its portfolio in liquid securities, which include 6.6% in cash and cash equivalents and 5.6% in government bonds, and 77.5% in corporate bonds. Because of our screening, our sample funds are disproportionately focused on high-yield bonds. In addition, compared to the average corporate bond, the bond issues held by our sample funds are on average more liquid, younger, and larger (Table 1, panel C).

2.2 Funds’ position size and its determinants

Table 1, panel B, reports descriptive statistics for the funds’ corporate bond positions, with the first five columns again highlighting the main sample (11,312,852 fund-bond-reporting period observations). Funds turnover about 17% of their existing positions in a month, which is equivalent to about 200% over a year. Conditional on holding a particular bond at the beginning of the period, the funds increase and decrease the position 6% and 11% of the times, respectively. The average change in position is a decrease of 4.6%.

Just like the fund’s TNA, the fund’s position size in a bond, relative to the bond issue size, is positively skewed with an average of 0.6%, a median of only 0.2%, and a 90th percentile of 1.5%. Summing across all funds in the same family, the family’s position size is about 2–3 times as large (equivalent to 2–3 funds in the same family holding the same bond) and is similarly positively skewed.

In the cross-section, the main determinant of fund position size is the fund’s asset size. The logarithm of the funds’ TNA explains 47% of the variation in funds’ average position size and 46% of the variation in the fraction of a fund’s portfolio that consists of positions in the top size decile (LARGEFRAC). All other fund characteristics inTable 1, panel A, collectively explain just another 8%–9% of the variation in LARGEFRAC. Nonetheless, there is significant overlap in position size between small and large funds. For example, if we split the sample into funds with TNA above and below the median on each report date, we find that the 25th percentile of position size for funds with TNA above the median is about the same as the 75th percentile of position size for funds with TNA below the median. The impact of larger funds trading in their small positions can thus be thought of as similar to that of smaller funds as in our simple framework.

Interestingly, the average share of an outstanding bond issue, as well as the fraction of positions that are in the top decile of shares of an outstanding bond issue, in the portfolios of funds with TNA above the median, increases by about 5%–10% following periods of large individual or aggregate outflows and reverts to the mean in about 6 months. This is consistent with the idea that during periods of turmoil, funds avoid causing negative price spillovers, but also suggests that funds aim for a more diversified portfolio and sell their large positions in normal times.

2.3 Bond-level data and ownership concentration

FollowingBessembinder et al. (2018)andAnand, Jotikasthira, and Venkataraman (2021), we consider only bonds in the FISD database that are classified as nonputtable U.S. Corporate Debentures and U.S. Corporate Bank Notes (bond type-CDEB or USBN). Our sample includes a total of 32,006 distinct bond issues (CUSIPs), which are held by mutual funds at some point in time.Table 1, panel C, reports descriptive statistics on the characteristics of these bonds, with the first five columns again highlighting the main sample (1,514,632 bond-month observations). Approximately 67% of the bond-month observations are for investment-grade bonds.6Together, all taxable mutual funds own about 10% of the average bond issue in our sample.

Throughout the analysis, we measure the incentives of the mutual fund owners to internalize the negative price spillovers of their trades using the proportion of the outstanding bond issue held by the top mutual fund owner.7The reason for doing so, instead of considering the topN(N> 1) owners, is that the median bond issue is held by 15 mutual funds, with the median share of the outstanding bond issue equal to 0.2%. Thus, while most owners are very small, the share of the top fund exhibits significantly higher variation and is skewed as are the funds’ TNAs: On average, the top mutual fund owner holds 3.5% of a bond issue and the 10th and 90th percentiles of the top fund ownership are 0.4% and 8.0%, respectively. The share of the top mutual fund owner is thus the main determinant of how concentrated the ownership of a bond issue is. While our main results are robust if we consider ownership of the top-two mutual funds (Table IA.4), the share of the outstanding bond issue owned by all mutual funds and the share of the top-three or more mutual funds are quasi-multicollinear with a coefficient of correlation of 91% or higher, implying that their effects cannot be independently identified.

Unsurprisingly, the top mutual fund owners have larger shares in smaller issues. Top mutual fund ownership is also higher in issues with a higher proportion of mutual fund ownership in general. Given the size of the top owner’s shares, ownership concentration should not lead to lower liquidity. Nevertheless, to make sure that differences in liquidity do not drive our findings, we control for bond turnover, fraction of zero trading days, effective bid ask spread, all computed in a rolling window of 6 months, as well as the relative liquidity of a bond in a fund’s portfolio.

For each bond-month, we calculate the return as the prorated coupon plus the change in value-weighted average price (VWAP) of the bond from the last day on which there are transactions in the previous month to the last day on which there are transactions in the current month, divided by the VWAP on the last day of the previous month. We compute returns only if the VWAP can be calculated in the last 10 days of each month. A total of 1,063,873 bond-month observations have nonmissing returns. The average bond return in our sample is about 29 basis points per month. Bond returns vary significantly across bonds and over time, with the 10th and 90th percentiles equal to –2.0% and 2.8% per month, respectively.

We define potential flow-induced fire sales (FIFS) similarly toEdmans, Goldstein, and Jiang (2012). Specifically, FIFSs aim to measure the notional selling pressure that a bond would experience if the mutual funds owners liquidated their portfolio pro rata when they experience large redemptions (flows in the bottom decile). Coherently with our measure of mutual funds’ ownership concentration, we weigh the extreme outflows (flows in the bottom decile) by the share of the outstanding bond issue owned by the mutual funds experiencing outflows.8FIFS appears smaller in magnitude for the corporate bonds in our sample than for the stocks inEdmans, Goldstein, and Jiang (2012)for two reasons. First, aggregate mutual fund ownership is smaller for bonds than for stocks. Second, we use monthly flows, whileEdmans, Goldstein, and Jiang (2012)use quarterly flows.

2.4 The COVID-19 sample

The last column ofTable 1, panels A, B, and C, reports the means of fund-, position-, and bond-level variables for our COVID-19 sample, which focuses on the period following the Fed intervention (April 2020 to September 2020). The sample criteria are the same as those used to create the main sample, except that we do not apply the 50% threshold for the portfolio share of corporate bonds. Instead, to have more statistical power over a short sample period, we keep all bond funds that hold corporate bonds.

Mutual fund characteristics, such as percentage cash and government bond allocations, differ from those of the main sample because we do not impose the 50% threshold for the portfolio share of corporate bonds. The post-Fed-intervention period is otherwise similar to the main sample along most dimensions, except that the average fund and bond returns exhibit a strong rebound on average.
article.3 Portfolio Liquidation and Ownership Co:
3.1 Main results

The first implication of our theoretical framework is that funds sell the bonds in which they hold large shares of the outstanding issue to a lower extent when redemptions and flow-driven asset sales occur. We thus consider periods in which the aggregate outflows from bond mutual funds are in the top decile of our sample and estimate the following fund ( f )-bond ( b )-time ( t ) level equation: α β ε Position change ( f , b , t ) = α + β × Top decile o f fund position siz e f , b , t + Δ b , t + γ f , t + ε f , b , t . (1)

Our variable of interest,Top decile of fund position sizef,b,t, equals one if a position is in the top decile of our fund-bond-time data set, and zero otherwise. Consistent with the implications of our simple framework, the dependent variable is defined as the percentage change in a fund’s holdings. We expect thatββ=0if mutual funds liquidate their positions pro rata. If instead mutual funds internalize the negative spillovers of their trades, we expectββ>0⁠. By including the interactions of fund and time fixed effects,γf,t⁠, we absorb any time-varying fund characteristics, including cash holdings and extent of redemptions, and evaluate which positions a fund chooses to liquidate to a larger extent within its portfolio. Depending on the specification, the matrix,Δb,t⁠, includes either time-varying controls for bond characteristics, such as ownership by insurance companies, measures of liquidity including turnover, bid-ask spread, fraction of zero trading days (all computed in the prior 6 months), bond age, and issue size, or interactions of bond issue and time fixed effects.

Table 2clearly shows that funds that hold a large share of an outstanding issue decrease their position in that issue to a lower extent during periods of generalized fire sales. For example, the estimates in column 1 ofTable 2suggest that when the aggregate outflows are in the top decile of our sample, funds decrease positions in which their share of the outstanding issue is in the top decile of our fund-bond-time sample by about 1.46% less than otherwise similar positions in which they own a smaller share of the outstanding issue (relative to the mean position change of –5.20%). The results are quantitatively and qualitatively similar in column 4, where we absorb bond heterogeneity by including the interactions of bond issue and time fixed effects.

The estimates demonstrate that mutual funds with a large share of an outstanding bond issue sell that bond issue less than other mutual fund owners, regardless of its liquidity and other time-varying characteristics. The effect appears to be largely driven by high-yield bonds (columns 3 and 6). This is consistent with the implications of our theoretical framework because investment-grade bonds are more liquid, and sales have smaller negative effects on their price. Internalizing such effects, fund managers sell high-yield bonds in which they hold large shares to a lower extent when they experience negative shocks. Transaction costs cannot explain the trading pattern we highlight because trade size is negatively related to execution costs, as documented in previous literature (e.g.,Edwards, Harris, and Piwowar 2007) as well as in our sample.9

While the coefficient forTop decile of fund position sizeis not statistically significant for investment-grade corporate bonds in columns 2 and 5, further examination shows that funds try to stabilize the prices of these bonds too. Funds that hold mostly investment-grade corporate bonds also hold significant quantities of other investment-grade bonds, such as Treasuries. Since these other investment-grade bonds are more liquid, investment-grade bond funds can liquidate them to meet redemptions without selling their corporate bonds at fire-sale prices. However, in turbulent times, when they experience very large redemptions, funds find it optimal to liquidate their more illiquid positions, including their corporate bonds (Jiang, Li, and Wang 2021). In Table IA.1, we focus on subsamples in which funds are likely to have scarce liquid assets and show that the way in which funds liquidate their corporate bonds is consistent with our theoretical framework. When redemptions are particularly large, for instance, if aggregate outflows are in the top 5% (as opposed to top 10% in columns 1–3), or if the funds allocate at least 60% in corporate bonds (as opposed to 50% in columns 4–6) and have therefore less liquid assets, the coefficients for theTop decile of fund position sizeare generally larger and statistically significant for both investment-grade and high-yield bonds.

Overall, the inclusion of bond-time and fund-time fixed effects (columns 4–6) allows us to dismiss alternative explanations that are based on bond or fund omitted characteristics. However, one may wonder whether large positions in a bond issue are the least liquid in a given fund’s portfolio, which could explain why fund managers liquidate these positions to a lower extent independently from their propensity to internalize externalities. To evaluate the merit of this alternative explanation, we control for the decile of turnover of a given bond issue within a fund’s portfolio. Consistent with the findings ofMa, Xiao, and Zeng (2022), in column 7, we indeed find that fund managers liquidate their most liquid assets disproportionately more. However, this leaves the coefficient for our variable of interest unaffected suggesting that an alternative explanation based on bond (relative) liquidity does not drive our findings.

3.2 Mechanisms

Table 3corroborates the interpretation of our findings by testing the mechanisms behind our hypotheses. First, evidence of stabilizing trading behavior should be driven by funds that need to liquidate their corporate bonds because they have limited cash and treasuries, which can be liquidated with negligible price impact.10Column 1 tests whether this is the case by including in Equation (3.1) an interaction term betweenTop decile of fund position sizeand a dummy that takes value equal to one for funds that at timet—1 have a proportion of TNA in cash and other liquid assets in the bottom two-thirds. The interaction term is positive and significant while the direct effect ofTop decile of fund position sizeloses statistical significance. This indicates that indeed the mechanism we propose is most relevant for funds that are unable to use only liquid assets to meet redemptions and need to choose which illiquid assets to liquidate.

In column 2, we consider that negative spillovers should be larger for the more illiquid bonds. Our model predicts that funds that own a large position in an illiquid bond issue should limit the sales of this security to an even larger extent. This is precisely what we observe when we interactTop decile of fund position sizewith a dummy that takes value equal to one if the effective spread of a bond is in the top two-thirds within the fund’s portfolio.

Our results so far show that mutual funds internalize the negative price spillovers, which as illustrated by our model, occur both through the sale proceeds and the remaining positions in a bond. In column 3, we attempt to isolate the importance of the latter.

FollowingChernenko and Sunderam (2020), we add to Equation (3.1) a dummy that equals one if the aggregate position in the bond of the other funds in the same family is in the top decile of our sample. The dummy captures the consideration of price spillovers on the (unsold) positions of other funds in the same family. Our estimates show that the position size of the other funds in the family is almost as important as the position size of the fund itself, which suggests that in choosing which bonds to liquidate, funds consider not only the effect of the price spillovers on the sale proceeds but also the value of the unsold positions.

Overall, these tests support our hypothesis that fund managers with large shares of outstanding bond issues behave strategically to limit the negative price spillovers of their forced sales and the consequent negative feedback effects on their net total assets.

3.3 Controlling for redemptions

So far, we have focused on periods of large aggregate redemptions, which are those that raise most concerns for financial stability. However, large mutual funds, which tend to have more positions in the top decile, could experience less outflows and this, rather than their propensity to internalize negative price spillovers, could explain why they sell to a lower extent the positions which represent a large share of an outstanding bond issue. To address this concern, in Table 4 , we estimate a specification similar to the one used by Ma, Xiao, and Zeng (2022) , which allows us to evaluate how funds liquidate their bond portfolios, holding constant the redemptions they experience. Specifically, we examine whether the sensitivity of fund f ’s change in bond b holdings to the outflows it experiences at time t is reduced if fund f holds a large position in bond b . Columns 1 to 3 provide evidence in support of our hypothesis. The estimates imply that in response to a 1% outflow, bond funds decrease their average corporate bond positions by about 0.76%–0.82%, during periods of large outflows from the bond mutual fund industry. The coefficients for the interaction ofOutflowandTop decile of fund position sizeshow that such propensity decreases by 0.12%–0.27% when the fund owns a large share of an outstanding bond issue.11Also, in column 4, the result is qualitatively and quantitatively invariant if we control for the liquidity decile rank of the bond within the portfolio of the fund experiencing redemptions by including an interaction between the turnover decile rank of a bond issue in the fund’s portfolio and the outflows from the fund. Our results are also robust if we consider only funds that experience large redemptions, as captured by funds with monthly flows in the bottom quintile of the sample (columns 5 to 8).12

3.4 Additional robustness checks

A potential alternative explanation for our findings is that funds expect better performance from the securities in which they hold a large share of the outstanding issue. To differentiate between this alternative explanation and the internalization of price externalities, we exploit exogenous variation inTop decile of fund position sizearising from fund mergers, because these large shares are less likely to be active bets of the fund managers.13We focus on the 12 months after the merger and consider as affected by the merger (Treated) positions that are held by both merging funds, while for any bonds that were held by only one of the funds theTreateddummy is set equal to zero. Our instrument is theTreateddummy interacted with the logarithm of the fund’s post-mergerTNA.Table 5shows that the instrument is indeed positively associated withTop decile of fund position sizeand that the F-test statistics for weak instrument is 30.72. Since the instrument is not weak, we present the second stage estimates, which fully support our earlier findings. The significantly larger coefficient forTop decile of fund position sizein the 2SLS estimates in comparison to the corresponding coefficient inTable 2is likely to be driven by a local average treatment effect, because the large shares of outstanding bond issues are significantly larger in this sample, and, consequently, the incentives to internalize externalities should be stronger.

Finally, based on our theoretical framework, we expect funds that own a large share of an outstanding issue to have particularly strong incentives to internalize the negative spillovers of their trades when large redemptions occur. Not only are sales in other periods more likely to be information driven but also the negative price spillovers of the sales should be smaller. It is thus comforting that in Table IA.2, when we consider periods with nonnegative aggregate flows into the bond mutual funds’ industry and exclude any observations of funds that experience negative flows, the estimated coefficients in columns 1 and 3 are smaller in magnitude and have lower statistical significance in comparison to those inTable 2.

3.5 Mutual funds’ trading and the SMCCF

In this section, we focus on the turbulence in the corporate bond market caused by the COVID-19 shock. We study this period separately because the intervention of the Fed, through the SMCCF on March 23, 2020, limited the expected price externalities of distressed sales for some corporate bonds more than for others (Haddad, Moreira, and Muir 2021). This implies that our hypothesis generates more detailed testable implications for this period than for other episodes of large redemptions from the bond mutual fund industry.

On March 23, 2020, to avert a crisis in the bond market, the Fed, together with other policy interventions, announced the purchases of investment-grade corporate bonds with less than 5-year remaining maturity implicitly providing a price backstop for these securities. The announcement made the demand for eligible investment-grade bonds more elastic and fund managers should have been able to sell them without worrying much about the price impact. After the announcement, they should thus have focused their stabilization efforts on ineligible bonds.

To test these hypotheses, we estimate model (1) after the Fed’s announcement over different sets of securities.14We ideally would have wanted to test that funds sell to a lower extent all categories of bonds in which they held large shares before the Fed’s announcement. Unfortunately, we are unable to test this hypothesis with monthly data because positions at the end of March reflect the effects of the Fed’s intervention, which swiftly calmed markets and may have led funds to repurchase the most undervalued bonds.15

In Panel A ofTable 6, we thus focus on funds’ trading behavior in the 6 months starting after March 2020. Since the negative spillovers of fire sales were significantly reduced for SMCCF-eligible bonds, funds stopped stabilizing these bonds. In fact, in column 2, funds with large shares in eligible bond issues sold these issues to a larger extent. This may be because many funds had already sold their small positions and started to sell their larger positions in relatively more liquid SMCCF-eligible corporate bonds, which would be consistent with our theoretical framework. In addition, the tendency to sell small positions makes a fund’s portfolio less diversified. The propensity to sell eligible bonds that are in the top decile of fund position size may suggest that funds value diversification and that the stabilizing behavior we highlight has costs in terms of portfolio under-diversification, which would be consistent with the descriptive evidence we discuss in Subsection 2.2.

In contrast, mutual funds facing large outflows continued to sell more cautiously both ineligible investment-grade and high-yield bonds in which they had large ownership shares. They decreased positions in SMCCF-ineligible investment-grade bonds in which their ownership shares ranked in the top decile by 2.73% less than otherwise similar but smaller positions. As a benchmark for comparison, the mean position change is –6.11% in this sample. The economic effect is somewhat larger in magnitude for SMCCF-ineligible high-yield bonds in column 8.

To further isolate the effect of negative spillovers of fire sales on trading behavior and abstract from unobserved heterogeneity, since investment-grade bond eligibility depended on bond maturity, we consider as a control sample for ineligible investment-grade bonds, eligible bonds with maturity just below the cutoff. It would be comforting if as we restrict bond maturities around the eligibility cutoff limiting unobserved heterogeneity (correlated with maturity), we observed that the effect of a fund’s large position became stronger for ineligible bonds.16In panel B, the coefficient forTop decile of fund position sizebecomes larger as we restrict the sample to bonds with maturity closer to the 5-year eligibility cutoff. Importantly, the coefficients for the interaction betweenTop decile of fund position size x Fed eligibleare negative and significant in all subsamples, and the magnitudes largely negate the main effects of fund position size being in the top decile. These results support our earlier conclusions that the stabilizing trading behavior inTable 2is driven by funds’ concerns about the negative spillovers of their sales and disappears for largely similar bonds for which the Fed provided a price backstop.17
article.4 Ownership Concentration and Bond Retur:
4.1 Returns

The hypothesis that bond funds internalize the price externalities of their trades when they own a large share of an outstanding bond issue implies that bonds with concentrated mutual fund ownership should experience less pronounced price declines when their owners experience outflows. Panel A ofTable 7provides a first evaluation of the evidence. Each month, we sort bonds in deciles of top mutual fund ownership. We present average returns for each decile of top mutual fund ownership, distinguishing between periods in which a security experiences large flow-induced fire sales and normal times. We define periods of flow-induced fire sales as bond-month observations in which the average of the outflows experienced by the mutual funds holding a particular bond issue, weighted using their ownership shares, which we label FIFS, is in the top decile of the sample. The returns of all bond portfolios appear negative and significantly lower when large redemptions occur in comparison to normal times. The differences in returns between bonds with ownership concentration in the bottom and top decile are economically and statistically significant only in periods of financial turmoil. On average, the returns of bonds in which the ownership of the top mutual fund is in the top decile outperform those in the bottom decile by an average of 0.61% per month, or 7.36% annualized.

The impact of ownership concentration is larger for high-yield bonds. High-yield bonds that are in the top decile for the ownership of the top mutual fund outperform those in the bottom decile by 0.95%, or 11.37% per annum during periods of turmoil. This is consistent with our earlier finding that managers’ propensity to internalize the externalities of their sales is more accentuated in high-yield bonds, which are relatively more illiquid. Mutual funds’ ownership concentration is unrelated to expected bond returns in normal times. This suggests that bond issues with higher mutual funds’ ownership concentration are not more illiquid because if this were the case, they would have to provide a premium to investors in the form of higher expected returns (Chen, Lesmond, and Wei 2007).

Panel B ofTable 7shows that our conclusions are robust when we control for other possible determinants of bond returns in a multivariate setting, including the bond issue’s liquidity, as captured by turnover, effective spread, and fraction of zero trading days. The specifications also control for the overall market movements using rating matched index returns and their interaction with (logged) bond maturity, allowing long maturity (or long duration) bonds to move more with the market. In addition, we include time and issuer fixed effects, and control for other bond characteristics that may affect returns, including (logged) bond maturity, (logged) bond issue size, (logged) bond age, a dummy variable for investment-grade bonds, insurance ownership, and dummy variables for bonds that are upgraded or downgraded in an interval of 2 months from the current month. In particular, the upgrade and downgrade dummies capture that information leading to a future rating change may be already available to market participants and affect their trading. The insurance ownership variable controls for the fact that insurance companies having stable liabilities tend to create less fire sales at least in investment-grade bonds (Barbosa and Ozdagli 2020;Coppola 2022). Finally, we control for bond mutual fund ownership, which according toMassa, Schumacher, and Wang (2021), may be lower in securities where mutual fund ownership concentration is higher. In our sample, however, higher mutual fund ownership is positively related to the proportion of the bond issue held by the top mutual fund.

We test whether a larger ownership share by the top mutual fund is associated with higher abnormal returns for bonds whose owners experience larger redemptions, as measured by FIFS. It is apparent that this is the case. The effect is again both statistically and economically significant. Based on the coefficient for the interaction term between FIFS andTop fund ownershipin column 2 of panel B, when bond issues experience FIFSs equal to the average of the largest 10% of our sample, increasing the top owner stake from 0.004 to 0.080 (ie, from the 10th to the 90th percentiles) increases the bond return by about 0.22% per month (4.803×0.006×(0.080–0.004)), or 2.63% annualized.18The estimated magnitudes are again larger for the less liquid high-yield bonds (column 4).

Importantly, in column 5, our findings for high-yield bonds are robust when we include the interactions of issuer and time fixed effects, which not only absorb any shocks to the issuer’s fundamentals but also control for the fact that an issuer may experience lower volatility because of its close relationships with intermediaries. Finally, we revisit the possibility that some bonds may be more fragile than others because they are owned by intermediaries with illiquid portfolios using the proxy for fragility ofJiang et al. (2022). We not only include this proxy for latent fragility across specifications but also interact it with FIFS in column 6. Our results are again invariant confirming that ownership concentration limits a bond’s exposure to fire sales.

InTable 8, we revisit the concern that some bonds may have high top mutual fund ownership because they differ in unobserved dimensions, such as their expected performance. To obtain exogenous variation in top mutual fund ownership, we focus on bonds that are held by funds involved in mergers and consider their monthly returns in the year following the merger. We focus on mergers of large funds (funds with assets under management above 600 million, which is the 25th percentile of the merger sample) to consider mergers that are likely to lead to large changes in ownership. We instrument top mutual fund ownership using the ownership shares of the two merging funds before the merger. We set the instrument equal to zero if a bond was held by only one of the merging funds. Since ownership concentration is interacted with FIFS, we also interact our instrument with FIFS to obtain a second instrument and present the two first stages in columns 1 and 2. The F-test statistic of the excluded instruments shows that our instruments are not weak. Importantly, the coefficient for the interaction term between FIFS andTop fund ownershipin the second stage presented in column 3 is positive and significant, supporting our earlier conclusions. Also in this case, as inTable 5, using exogenous variation in mutual fund ownership concentration due to mergers increases the magnitude of the effect, possibly because we capture more extreme levels of ownership concentration.19

4.2 Bond returns following the Fed’s intervention through the SMCCF

Since as shown inTable 6, the Fed’s intervention in March 2020 affected the behavior of funds with a large share of an outstanding bond issue, we investigate whether the relationships between ownership concentration and bond returns were also affected by the Fed’s announcement, distinguishing between eligible and ineligible bonds.20

In Panel A ofTable 9, following the Fed’s intervention, ownership concentration is associated with higher returns for ineligible bonds that experience higher FIFSs. The estimates in column 3 indicate that when a bond’s owners experience FIFSs equal to the average of the largest 10% of the ineligible investment-grade bond sample, the bond’s price drops by 2.39% (⁠−3.991×0.006⁠). However, increasing the top ownership stake from 0.004 to 0.080 (ie, the 10th to the 90th percentiles) almost completely eliminates the negative effect of FIFS, reducing the magnitude of the price drop by as much as 2.06% (45.069×0.006×(0.080–0.004)). Similarly in the high-yield bond subsample in column 4, following FIFS equal to the average of the largest 10%, the bond’s price drops by 2.05% (–2.922×0.007); increasing the top ownership stake from 0.004 to 0.080 (ie, from the 10th to the 90th percentiles) reduces the magnitude of the price drop by 1.68% (36.912×0.007×(0.080–0.004)).

After the Fed’s intervention, ownership concentration is not positively associated with the returns of eligible bonds, that is, the bonds in which we no longer expect and observe the stabilizing trading behavior of fund managers with a large share of the outstanding issue. We even find a negative effect of top mutual fund ownership on the returns of bonds with larger FIFSs. This, together with the earlier finding that funds were selling their large positions in eligible bonds to a larger extent, may potentially be explained by fund managers overestimating the effect of the price backstop provided by the Fed on eligible bonds’ elasticity of demand. However, the negative effect of ownership concentration on the return of eligible bonds with owners subject to fire sales largely vanishes when we limit unobserved heterogeneity in panel B.

Panel B tests for differences in the effects of ownership concentration on the returns of eligible and ineligible investment-grade bonds subject to FIFS shocks, considering progressively narrower intervals around the 5-year maturity cutoff to reduce unobserved heterogeneity in bond characteristics. We continue to find that for ineligible investment-grade bonds, FIFSs result in price drops that are mitigated by ownership concentration, even if the coefficient is not statistically significant in column 3 due to the smaller sample size.

Importantly, in columns 2 and 3, where we rely on narrower intervals and limit bond heterogeneity to a larger extent, the coefficients forFIFS×Top fund ownership×Fed eligibleis negative and largely negates the effect ofFIFS×Top fund ownershipsuggesting that large owners do not engage in the stabilizing trading behavior, as we would expect, but at the same time do not drive down the returns of eligible bonds. In fact, the coefficients forFIFS×Fed eligibleare positive and highly statistically significant across the board, indicating that the Fed’s price backstop was effective, regardless of ownership concentration, as is consistent with our hypotheses.

Overall, these results support our conjecture that ownership concentration should only shield from FIFS shocks ineligible bonds, that is, the only securities that did not have a price backstop and that consequently large owners had reasons to sell to a lower extent.

4.3 Exposure to marketwide shocks

We have so far shown that when mutual funds experience large redemptions, bonds in which the top mutual fund owns a larger share of the outstanding issue are less negatively affected. In this section, we further show that this behavior shields the returns of bonds with more concentrated mutual fund ownership from systematic shocks, as negative realizations of market returns are typically accompanied by outflows from the bond mutual fund industry.

Table 10shows that bonds with higher top mutual fund ownership perform better in periods of more intense aggregate outflows from the mutual fund industry. The effect emerges clearly in column 2 when we consider investment-grade bonds and in column 4 for high-yield bonds, after we absorb unobserved heterogeneity by including interactions of issuer and time fixed effects. In column 5, we control for the proxy for fragility ofJiang et al. (2022), which we interact with the dummy capturing intense outflows from the bond mutual fund industry. Our results are robust.

The estimated effects are not only statistically, but also economically significant. For instance, the estimates in column 4 imply that in periods in which aggregate fund flows are in the bottom decile, high-yield bonds in the top decile of top fund ownership outperform those in the bottom decile by about 32 basis points per month (⁠0.0415×(0.080−0.004)⁠) or over 3.78% per year.

These results confirm that when many owners experience outflows, mutual funds that own a large proportion of an outstanding bond issue internalize the externalities of their trades. Consequently, the stabilizing trading behavior of funds with a large share of an outstanding bond issue shields bond prices from systematic shocks, which are typically accompanied by large redemptions and fire sales.
article.5 The Effects of Stabilizing Trading on:
5.1 Performance and flows of funds with large positions

This section explores whether the internalization of negative price spillovers benefits mutual funds’ performance. According to our theoretical framework, the strategic trading of HIGHLARGEFRAC funds should shield their portfolios from the negative spillovers of fire sales. As a result, these funds are expected to have superior performance when generalized fire sales occur.

We test whether funds whose portfolio comprises more positions that represent large shares of outstanding bond issues perform better by estimating the following equation: α β ε Alpha ( f , t ) = α + β × LARGEFRA C f , t − 1 + γ f , t − 1 + ε f , t , (3) where LARGEFRAC denotes the fraction of fund f ’s portfolio at the end of month t − 1 that consists of positions that are in the top size decile of the fund-bond-time sample. The vector γ f , t − 1 includes various time-varying fund controls, such as percentage cash holdings, (logged) TNA, and fund category x month fixed effects. We also include lagged flows and performance. Following Anand, Jotikasthira, and Venkataraman (2021) , we estimate Alpha from the four-factor model of Chen and Qin (2017) , using monthly observations over a rolling 18-month period.

Table 11reports the results. Funds that have incentives to avoid the negative price spillovers of fire sales in a larger fraction of their portfolio have better performance, especially during periods of large aggregate outflows. As in our earlier results, the effect is largely driven by high-yield bond funds, that is, by funds that invest in assets whose valuations suffer the most from the negative price spillovers of fire sales. The effects are statistically and economically significant. The estimate ofββin column 4, for example, shows that increasing LARGEFRAC from 0 to 0.665 (ie, from the 10th to the 90th percentiles) raises the fund’s alpha during periods of large aggregate outflows by 15.40 basis points (0.232×0.665). For funds focusing on high-yield bonds (column 6), the effects of an interdecile increase in LARGEFRAC are 23.42 basis points (0.319×0.735). The mean alpha in our sample is just –5.28 basis points.

The positive effect of LARGEFRAC on alpha during periods of aggregate outflows translate into a positive, but small effect of LARGEFRAC on unconditional alpha (columns 1–3). This aligns with our hypothesis that the advantages of internalizing price externalities become apparent during periods of turmoil.

Given that fund flows are influenced by past performance, the superior performance of funds with high LARGEFRAC during periods of turmoil reduces their outflows. This, in turn, decreases their vulnerability to forced sales and contributes to further enhancing their future performance. Additionally, if investors are aware that funds with high LARGEFRAC perform better and are less vulnerable to fire sales during turbulent periods, they should be less concerned about negative performance and thus their flow sensitivity to negative performance should be reduced. This effect would further enhance the stability of the assets under management of funds with high LARGEFRAC.

To evaluate whether this is the case, we adopt a specification similar Chen, Goldstein, and Jiang (2017), who show that the outflows of corporate bond funds are highly sensitive to negative performance and may results in investor runs and financial fragility.Table 12tests whether the flows of funds with higher LARGEFRAC are less sensitive to negative performance, which would imply that these funds are less financially fragile. To do so, we concentrate on periods in which sample funds experience negative alpha and include the fund’s alpha att−1and the interaction of the latter with the variable LARGEFRAC att−1in the equation for flows in montht. In column 1, the negative and significant coefficient for this interaction term indicates that funds whose portfolio consists of a large share of top-decile positions have indeed flows that are less sensitive to negative past performance. In economic terms, a decrease in alpha of 1% in montht−1leads to outflows of 0.29% in montht. An interdecile increase in LARGEFRAC lowers the sensitivity by as much as 0.18% (–0.272×0.665).

The coefficient for the interaction term is negative, but not statistically significant in column 2, where we consider mutual funds that focus on investment-grade bonds and are less exposed to the negative fire-sale spillovers. In column 3, for funds investing mostly in high-yield bonds, for which the potential negative spillovers of fire sales are larger, the coefficient of interest is negative, slightly larger in magnitude, and statistically significant. This is consistent with our earlier findings and underscores the greater benefit of funds’ strategic behavior in high-yield bonds.

Overall, these results show that funds with high LARGEFRAC experience better performance during periods of turmoil and have more stable assets under management. They are consequently less financially fragile. Since better performance limits second-round redemptions, the internalization of externalities by HIGHLARGEFRAC funds can benefit the whole mutual fund industry contributing to lower redemptions after the initial shock. In the next subsection, we test whether HIGHLARGEFRAC funds’ strategic behavior indeed mitigates fire-sale spillovers on peer funds.

5.2 Spillovers effects of HIGHLARGEFRAC funds

The stabilizing trading behavior of HIGHLARGEFRAC funds implies that the returns of bonds with concentrated mutual fund ownership are higher in periods of generalized fire sales. The funds whose portfolio overlaps to a larger extent with that of HIGHLARGEFRAC funds should therefore experience better performance. To test this hypothesis, we adapt the methodology ofFalato et al. (2021), who show that redemptions from a fund negatively affect the performance and flows of other funds that hold similar assets.

We test whether redemptions from funds whose portfolios comprise a larger share of top-decile positions have smaller negative effects than redemptions from other funds on the performance and flows of peer funds with overlapping portfolios. For each fund f in month t , we define two variables capturing the potential spillovers due to redemptions experienced by the two types of peers: HIGHLARGEFRAC funds and other funds. Specifically, we define: Peer selling pressur e f , t type = ∑ b FIF S b , t type , − f × w f , b , t − 1 , where type refers to funds with LARGEFRAC in the top decile of our fund-month sample ( type = HIGHLARGEFRAC ) and other funds ( type = LOWLARGEFRAC) , respectively; w f , b , t − 1 is the portfolio weight of bond b in the portfolio of the focal fund f in month t − 1 ⁠ , and FIF S b , t type , − f are the redemptions experienced by bond b ’s mutual fund owners of a given type , excluding fund f , in month t , weighted by their ownership share in the bond issue. 21

We estimate the following regression: α β β ε Alpha ( f , t ) o r Flow ( f , t ) = α + β 1 × Peer selling pressur e f , t HIGHLARGEFRAC + β 2 × Peer selling pressur e f , t LOWLARGEFRAC + γ f , t + δ f + θ t + ε f , t , (4) where γ f , t is a vector of time-varying fund controls, including lagged flows, lagged alpha, and percentage cash holding, etc, δ δ f denotes fund fixed effects, and θ θ t denotes month fixed effects. The existence of negative spillovers implies that β β 1 and β β 2 should be negative, but if funds with a larger fraction of top-decile positions contribute to financial stability, we expect the absolute value of β β 1 to be smaller than that of β β 2 ⁠ .

Table 13presents the results. Panel A considers a fund’s alpha. Columns 1–4 show that redemptions from HIGHLARGEFRAC peer funds do not appear to have a significant negative effect on the focal fund’s performance. Negative spillovers arise only because of redemptions from other peer funds and are mostly concentrated in high-yield bond funds, that is, in the funds in which the negative price spillovers of sales are expected to be larger in the absence of any stabilizing behavior.

Panel B, columns 1–4, show that similar considerations extend to fund flows. While redemptions from HIGHLARGEFRAC peer funds negatively affect the flows of the focal fund, the estimated coefficients are about a quarter to half of those estimated for other peer funds. This implies that redemptions from funds that are more likely to internalize the negative spillovers from their sales have significantly smaller negative spillovers on other funds’ flows.

This evidence, as well as the documented effects of top mutual fund ownership on bond returns, implies that funds’ stabilizing trading behavior affects the distribution of fire sales: the better performance of bonds with concentrated ownership and of the funds whose portfolios overlap with HIGHLARGEFRAC funds could arise not only from HIGHLARGEFRAC funds’ tendency to sell to a lower extent their large positions but also from the fact that HIGHLARGEFRAC funds have to instead sell more of their small positions to meet redemptions. In our simple framework, HIGHLARGEFRAC funds, being atomistic in the small positions they sell to a larger extent, do not create negative spillovers through their sales. It is an empirical question however whether this is the case.

To evaluate the extent to which the data support this implication, in column 5 of both panels, we split the peer selling pressure from HIGHLARGEFRAC funds into the pressures coming from their large versus small positions, respectively:Peersellingpressuref,LARGEPOS,tHIGHLARGEFRACandPeersellingpressuref,SMALLPOS,tHIGHLARGEFRAC⁠. If HIGHLARGEFRAC funds create volatility in the small positions they sell, we should observe that funds whose portfolio overlaps to a larger extent with small positions of HIGHLARGEFRAC funds perform worse and have more outflows whenPeersellingpressuref,SMALLPOS,tHIGHLARGEFRACis high. In both panels, our estimates show that the coefficient forPeersellingpressuref,SMALLPOS,tHIGHLARGEFRAChas the same magnitude as the coefficient forPeersellingpressuref,tLOWLARGEFRAC⁠, while the coefficient forPeersellingpressuref,LARGEPOS,tHIGHLARGEFRACis significantly smaller, supporting our previous findings. These results indicate that HIGHLARGEFRAC funds do not create larger negative spillovers than other funds when they trade in their small positions.

Taken together, the evidence we present suggests that funds with large shares of outstanding bond issues, by trading strategically, create fewer fire sales. In doing so, they experience better performance and stabilize their own assets under management, and they also limit the negative spillovers on the flows and performance of their peer funds.
article.6 Conclusion:
We show that mutual funds with large shares of outstanding bond issues trade strategically to minimize the negative price spillovers of flow-induced fire sales. These funds sell their holdings of the bond issues in which they hold large shares to a lower extent during periods of turmoil. As a result, funds with many large bond positions mitigate fire sales. In so doing, they experience better performance and stabilize their own assets under management, and they also limit the negative spillovers that redemptions from their funds have on the flows and performance of other funds with overlapping portfolios.

Our findings suggest that there exist stabilizing forces within the mutual fund industry when bond mutual funds must collectively contract their balance sheets, which has important policy implications. The Financial Stability Board has launched a consultation to consider funds with more than $100 billion in assets under management as systemically important financial institutions because large funds can take more risks and execute larger trades, which potentially increase asset volatility (Chen, Du, and Sun 2022).22However, our results imply that large funds, which naturally own larger shares of outstanding bond issues relative to small funds, enhance price stability compared to a myriad of small funds with correlated flows. Regulators should thus focus on more than large funds and be concerned about the systemic implications of small bond funds.
